course_description: Law relating to commercial companies; in particular, legal personality, directors' duties, shareholders' rights, liquidations, takeovers, & comparison with partnership structure.

requirements.prerequisites: COMM1201 or 1202 or LAWS1001 or 1100 or 1101
requirements.restrictions: Not to be studied by LLB or LLB dual students Quota: Summer semester minimum of 30 enrolments
requirements.incompatible: CO323 or 820 or LA304 or (311 + 312) or COMM3205 or 7202 or LAWS7011 or 5017 or 7117 or 7317
requirements.assumed_background: You will be assumed to have studied and passed some introductory law course and to have retained from that course an understanding of the Australian legal system; the ability to apply legal knowledge and legal reasoning in the analysis and resolution of legal problems; and the ability to independently research legal issues.

timetable:
Timetables are available on mySI-net.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims:
Teaching Mission Statement

The mission of the TC Beirne School of Law is to educate students in and about the law. We seek to assist you to think critically about the law and to investigate its structures, principles, policies, and values. This is achieved by engaging with you as fellow investigators in a range of critical and intellectual conversations about the law and its place in society. Our role is thus to guide you in your own intellectual journey in the law and to equip you with the necessary skills that will enable you to continue that journey long after you have left the School.

Course Aims

To equip you with sufficient knowledge and understanding of company and partnership law to enable you, upon graduation, to deal with business persons and legal advisers in an informed and reasoned manner.

To equip you with the knowledge and understanding required to solve legal problems relating to each of the topics in the course.

To demonstrate the development of legal principles through case law and statutory provisions.

To inspire you to approach the study of law with a keen and inquiring mind, and to maintain a life-long interest in company law issues.

learning_outcomes.description: Describe the regulatory framework applicable to companies and partnerships in Australia;
Compare and contrast the advantages and disadvantages of the corporate form with the partnership form of business organisation;

Solve legal problems/issues relating to: (i) the concept of limited liability; (ii) the types of companies formed under the Corporations Act 2001 and the significance of the various classifications; (iii) the separate legal entity principle; (iv) the corporate constitution; (v) corporate liability for contracts; (vi) directors duties; (vii) shareholders ability to enforce directors duties; (viii) the rights and consequences attaching to share ownership; (ix) corporate finance; (x) external administration; (xi) the relationship between partners and their liability to creditors of a partnership; and
Present coherent written argument on company and partnership law issues.

assessment_details.title: Research Paper
assessment_details.task_description:
This is an individual assessment item which requires you to firstly select a topic from a list of four (4) available on Blackboard - Assessment. The paper will be a maximum of 3,000 words. There will be a maximum of 35 students per topic. Students must nominate their selected topic no later than 10 December 2010. Topic nomination will be on-line and once the maximum number of students has been reached there will be no further additions. The Course C-oordinator will have the final say on topics and will notify you of any change to your selected topic no later than 13 December 2010.

Submission directions will be provided in Lectorials.

The paper will be marked as per the Criteria Sheet attached. You will note that there are four (4) marking criteria: structure, style, content and critical analysis.

Word Limit - the word limit will be strictly enforced. Words above 10% of the maximum word limit of 3,000 words will incur a penalty deduction of 1% per 30 words. Example - number of words 600 - penalty will be 10%.

assessment_details.title: Final Examination
assessment_details.task_description:
All material covered in the course is examinable in the final examination. The examination will test knowledge and understanding of the material covered in the lectorials and the prescribed text through the application of such knowledge and understanding:

to a given set of facts; and/or

in consideration of an essay/short answer question.

The final exam format will be 'OPEN BOOK'. You will be permitted to bring any material into the exam.

Any issues regarding the administration, timetabling and non-attendance at final examinations need to be directed to The University of Queensland Examinations section. These issues are not the responsibility of the Course Co-ordinator.

learning_resources.school_learning_resources:
Law School Citation Style

Students preparing assignments, research papers, dissertations and theses are required to follow the Australian Guide to Legal Citation (3rd edition) .

The Law Library has produced a How-to Guide to assist students to follow the AGLC3 citation style.

Law Library

The Law Library's Legal Research Guide site at http://www.library.uq.edu.au/law/lawsites.phtml provides links to a wide range of Australian and International sites relevant to the study of law.

Students should refer to the UQ Library website generally for information relating to facilities, collections and services available - http://www.library.uq.edu.au .

Course Blackboard Website

Where the course utilises Blackboard , the Blackboard website will contain: course announcements; the course profile; the learning guide (where applicable); lecture slides; discussion boards; and intra-course email facilities. Students should visit the course Blackboard website regularly to check for course announcements and to read and contribute to any online activities.

The Course Coordinator will maintain and regularly update the course Blackboard website, which can be accessed via the following link http://blackboard.elearn.uq.edu.au . It is your responsibility to ensure that you have access to the website . Access to Blackboard is automatically generated for those students enrolled through my SI-net approximately 1 week prior to the commencement of classes. If you do not have access in the first week of classes please contact the Law School Webmaster by email webmaster@law.uq.edu.au .

AskIT , available through the University of Queensland Library, offers computing help and training free to UQ students. Contact the AskIT desk at Level 2 of the Duhig Building at St Lucia, or Building 8 at Ipswich - http://askit.uq.edu.au .
learning_resources.other_learning_resources:
The Discussion Board on Blackboard is for discussion between students and will be monitored by the Course Co-ordinator. The Discussion Board may also be used by the course co-ordinator to enhance knowledge of certain content areas as well as provide non-assessable activities for the student's further understanding.

The Discussion Board IS NOT a replacement for attending Lectorials during the Semester.

Responses by the Course Co-ordinator WILL NOT necessarily be direct answers but may direct you to further resources.

Previous 2. Aims

Next 4. Activities

learning_activities.topic: Topic 1 - Introduction to LAWS3100 (Lectorial): This topic will introduce the course content. It will provide students with a summary of the company and partnership structure including disadvantages and advantages. Readings/Ref: Hanrahan (Chap 1 ); P'Ship Act; Lguide (Topic 1); Business law; Websites

Topic 2 - The corporate form (Lectorial): Consider the most important characteristics of the corporate form; types of companies and the nature of corporate groups. We will also look briefly at the process of registering a company and listing a company on the stock exchange. Readings/Ref: Hanrahan (Chap 3); Lguide (Topic 2); Websites

Topic 3 - Introduction to corporate governance (Lectorial): Consider the way in which a company is managed and controlled using the company's internal governance rules and the law. Readings/Ref: Hanrahan (Chap 4); Lguide (Topic 3); Websites

Topic 4 - Members (Lectorial): Consider the owners of the business, the members; how they make decisions; how their meetings are conducted; what they are entitled to make decisions about; some restrictions on their decision making power and what remedies are available to them. Readings/Ref: Hanrahan (Chap 6); Lguide (Topic 4); Websites

Topic 5 - Directors (Lectorial): Learn about how directors make decisions; different types of directors; appointment and removal; directors' meetings. We also consider directors' duties and the consequences of breaching their duties including insolvent trading. Readings/Ref: Hanrahan (Chap 10); Lguide (Topic 5); Websites

Topic 6 - Corporate finance and liability (Lectorial): Discussion on how companies are financed. We begin with an overview of the concept of corporate finance and consider some of the sources of corporate finance - share capital and debt capital. We will learn about the nature of share cpaital, how shares are issued and how a company must maintain and can increase levels of share capital. We will also learn about debt capital and company charges. Readings/Ref: Hanrahan (Chap 17); Lguide (Topic 7)
Topic 7 - Corporate contracting (Lectorial): We will look at the liability of companies: for contracts, for torts and for crimes. Readings/Ref: Hanrahan (Chap 22); Lguide (Topic 8); Websites

Topic 8 - External administration 1 (Lectorial): We will discuss how a company goes into some form of external administration. We will discuss three types of external administration namely, receivership, voluntary administration, and winding-up or liquidation. Readings/Ref: Hanrahan (Chap 25); Lguide (Topic 10); Websites

Topic 9 - External administration 2 (Lectorial): This topic examines liquidation (winding-up). We will consider voluntary liquidation by members; by creditors; and winding-up by a court. Readings/Ref: Hanrahan ( Chap 26); Lguide (Topic 11); Websites

Topic 10 - Other business structures (Lectorial): This topic will compare the corporate structure with that of a partnership, trust, and joint venture. We will discuss issues of formality, purpose, set-up costs and legal liability. This session will also provide a summary of the final exam.

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the Universitys assessment-related policies can be found on myAdvisor ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2910 ).

Academic Integrity
It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Plagiarism
The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Academic Integrity and Plagiarism policy ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25128 ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Feedback on Assessment
Feedback is essential to effective learning and students can expect to receive appropriate and timely feedback on all assessment. For a detailed explanation of the feedback you are entitled to, you should consult the policy on Student Access to Feedback on Assessment. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25109 )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the myAdvisor web site. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2953 )

School of Law
Assessment Guidelines

Communication with Examiners

Students who have completed an item of assessment should not contact the examiners about that item of assessment prior to the release of the grades or marks for that item of assessment. For queries of an administrative nature, please contact Law School Reception.

After the release of the final grades for the course, feedback may be sought on examination performance in accordance with the Schools policy ( http://www.law.uq.edu.au/obtaining-feedback-on-final-examinations).

Special Examinations

Centrally Controlled Special Examinations

Students should refer to myAdvisor for the policy relating to centrally controlled special examinations - http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2910

School Special Examinations

Where a student does not attend a mid-semester examination, the student will in the normal course of events receive zero marks for the examination.

Where, due to illness or other medical condition occurring on the day of the examination, a student is unable to attend a mid-semester examination, or the performance of a student in the examination has been impaired, the student may, on the presentation of a certificate covering the date of the examination from a registered medical practitioner and a written explanation of how the illness or other medical condition prevented attendance/impaired performance at the examination, be permitted to sit a special examination in lieu.

For full details, students should refer to the School policy on Absences from Mid-Semester Examinations .

Applications for a special mid-semester examination shall be made by lodging the Application for Special Mid-Semester Examination form (and supporting documentation) with the Law School Office within 5 days following the date of the mid-semester examination.

Special mid-semester examinations will be held in the period nominated by the School for the holding of mid-semester special examinations and may take the format of an oral examination.

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses
Students on a placement course also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice should refer to the University policy, Placement Courses ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25120 ) for detailed information.

Working with Children
Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25004 ) to find out how to apply for a blue card.

Students with a Disability
Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Adviser at Student Support Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25122 ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25111

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety
Undergraduate Students ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25055 ) and Postgraduate Students ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25057 ) should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory.

Other
School of Law
Guidelines

Responsibilities of Students

Students are expected to take responsibility for their own learning and academic development. This includes:

devoting sufficient time and effort to your studies to make and maintain adequate progress,

preparing for lectures and tutorials,

reading prescribed materials,

undertaking such independent study as necessary to reach an understanding of the materials,

completion and submission of assessment items on time,

compliance with School, Faculty, and University rules.

Student Workload

For each 2 unit course students are expected to undertake at least 7 8 hours per week of independent study in addition to the time spent in lectures and tutorials.

Student and Staff Communication

If you email a staff member directly, you must include in your email your name, your student number and the name of the course you are enquiring about. Courtesy is always appreciated.

If you telephone a staff member directly and leave a message on their voicemail, you must provide contact details other than a mobile telephone number except where this is absolutely unavoidable.

While the use of telephone and email is acceptable for brief communications with a staff member, you should not expect detailed responses to questions about the law. These questions should be raised on the discussion board, in tutorial sessions or in staff consultation sessions.

Law School Website

Students should refer to the Law School website for more information including enrolment information and important dates; student announcements; news and events; information on Law School prizes and scholarships etc http://www.law.uq.edu.au .

Previous 5. Assessment

Next Summary
course_introduction:
This course is a critical analysis of corporations and their regulation in Australia. You will learn about the nature of companies and company law; the corporate form; corporate governance; directors' duties; shareholders or members' rights; corporate finance; corporate liability; external administration; liquidation; and compare the corporate form with that of partnership. This course is delivered in intensive mode. Assessment will be based on a research paper and a final exam. (Please refer to Assessment for details). You will be expected to access data bases and other on-line sources to enhance your understanding of the course content as well as being able to apply both the law and your knowledge to case study and problem based scenarios. Their is an expectation that you have had some exposure to the subject of Business Law in Australia.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

This will be the first time that this course will be delivered by Lectorials. Lectorials are explained in section 4.2 of the ECP.

Students are encouraged to provide regular feedback to the Course Co-ordinator either on the Discussion Board or by email directly.

other_learning_activities_information:
The Function of Lectorials

The delivery of this course is being conducted as a combined Lecture/Tutorial, known as a Lectorial .

The format will be a mixture of:

Exploring concepts of law relevant to the topic;

Exploring how the particular concepts of law work, and relate to other concepts learned;

Identify the central conceptual, intellectual, and doctrinal questions raised by the material under consideration;

Develop skills in legal reasoning and argument;

Develop skills in using knowledge to critically analyse legal concepts; and

To think about legal issues from an objective viewpoint.

The sessions will not:-

Deliver an exclusive and exhaustive account of the material that the student needs to learn; or

Repeat or deliver reading material that students can read for themselves.

The sessions aim to link learning outcomes with course content and knowledge.

The Function of Seminars

Seminars are provided to further develop the student's understanding of the course content. They will be interactive sessions where students will work in groups to develop their own understanding through the use of different learning techniques. Much of the sessions will be peer led and the aim is to increase your knowledge and enhance your application of the relevant legal concept.

Seminars will be conducted prior to the final exam. As the Exam timetable has yet to be set for Summer Semester students will have a voice in the times for these Seminars.

course_grading:
Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Fail: Fails to demonstrate most or all of the basic requirements of the course:

1% - 19%

Grade 2, Fail: Demonstrates clear deficiencies in understanding and applying fundamental concepts; communicates information or ideas in ways that are frequently incomplete or confusing and give little attention to the conventions of the discipline:

20% - 47%

Grade 3, Fail: Demonstrates superficial or partial or faulty understanding of the fundamental concepts of the field of study and limited ability to apply these concepts; presents undeveloped or inappropriate or unsupported arguments; communicates information or ideas with lack of clarity and inconsistent adherence to the conventions of the discipline:

48% - 49%

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrates adequate understanding and application of the fundamental concepts of the field of study; develops routine arguments or decisions and provides acceptable justification; communicates information and ideas adequately in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

50% - 64%

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrates substantial understanding of fundamental concepts of the field of study and ability to apply these concepts in a variety of contexts; develops or adapts convincing arguments and provides coherent justification; communicates information and ideas clearly and fluently in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

65% - 74%

Grade 6, Distinction: As for 5, with frequent evidence of originality in defining and analysing issues or problems and in creating solutions; uses a level, style and means of communication appropriate to the discipline and the audience:

75% - 84%

Grade 7, High Distinction: As for 6, with consistent evidence of substantial originality and insight in identifying, generating and communicating competing arguments, perspectives or problem solving approaches; critically evaluates problems, their solutions and implications:

85% +
late_submission:
Where a non-examination item of assessment (including essays, assignments, take-home tests, etc) is not submitted by the due date and time, a penalty will be levied at the rate of 10% of the marks available for the assessment item per day (or part thereof) that the assessment is overdue.

Assessment submitted more than 5 days after the due date will receive zero marks. For the purposes of clarification, if the due date is the 10th, any work submitted on the 16th or later will receive zero marks. If the 6th day following the due date is a Saturday or a Sunday, assessment will be accepted until 9am on the following Monday.

No extension granted may exceed 14 days.

For full details, students should refer to the Universitys policy on Late Submission of Progressive Assessment . Except in exceptional circumstances, extensions for progressive items of assessment will only be considered in respect of illness or other certified medical condition . Applications for extension shall be made by lodging the Application for Extension of Progressive Assessment form (and supporting documentation) at the Law School Office by the due date for assessment unless the exceptional circumstance, illness or other medical condition is such that the student cannot reasonably be expected to have applied prior to the due date.

Students who are granted an extension beyond the due date for assessment items must submit their assessment to the Law School Office, NOT the BEL Faculty Collaborative Learning Centre.
